title: Excess mortality: Raw number of deaths from all causes compared to projection based on previous years
subtitle: The reported number of weekly or monthly deaths in 2020–2024 and the projected number of deaths for 2020, which is based on the reported deaths in 2015–2019.
caption:
Excess mortality: Raw number of deaths from all causes compared to projection based on previous years

The reported number of weekly or monthly deaths in 2020–2024 and the projected number of deaths for 2020, which is based on the reported deaths in 2015–2019.

This line chart tracks the raw number of weekly deaths in the United States from early 2020 through early 2021, comparing actual reported figures against a historical baseline. The vertical axis measures death counts ranging from 0 to 100,000, while the horizontal axis spans from January 5, 2020, to January 3, 2021. A black line labeled "Projected, 2020" establishes the expected trend based on data from 2015 through 2019, hovering consistently between 50,000 and 60,000 deaths per week. Faint gray lines representing individual years from 2015 to 2019 cluster tightly around this projection, illustrating typical seasonal variation. In contrast, the colored lines for 2020, 2021, 2022, and 2023 deviate significantly from this baseline at various points. The orange line for 2020 exhibits two distinct peaks that rise well above the projection: the first occurs around April 2020, reaching nearly 80,000 deaths, and the second begins in late 2020, climbing past 80,000 by the end of the charted period. The purple line for 2021 starts at a high point above 80,000 in January before declining to join the baseline cluster by mid-year, then rising again in the autumn to approximately 75,000. The teal line for 2022 and the blue line for 2023 generally track closer to the historical range but show periods of elevation, particularly in the winter months where they separate from the lower gray lines. The note indicates that the reported number of deaths might not count all deaths that occurred due to incomplete coverage and delays in reporting.
